invariant_definition: Inference — the *pramāṇa* that produces cognition of an unperceived object on the basis of an invariable relation (*vyāpti*) between a known mark (*liṅga*) and the inferred object (*sādhya*). The Nyāya five-membered syllogism (*pratijñā*, *hetu*, *udāharaṇa*, *upanaya*, *nigamana*) is the standard form. All Vedāntic schools accept *anumāna* as a *pramāṇa*; the schools differ on whether *anumāna* can establish *brahman* (no school says yes; *brahman* requires *śabda*) and on the technical analysis of inference (Dvaita develops a distinctive treatment in Vyāsatīrtha's *Tarka-Tāṇḍava*).

per_school.definition: *Anumāna* operates on its proper objects (e.g., the existence of fire from smoke); cannot establish *brahman*; can refute opposing positions (Sāṅkhya-pradhāna, etc.) by showing internal incoherence.

*Anumāna* receives a sophisticated treatment in the post-Madhva tradition (Jayatīrtha, Vyāsatīrtha) responding to Navya-Nyāya. *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* is the principal Dvaita treatise on inference and a major contribution to Indian logic.

translator_note: 'Inference' is the standard gloss. The Indian theory of inference (especially the *vyāpti*-grounded structure) is technically distinct from the Aristotelian syllogism — *anumāna* requires an empirically established invariable concomitance, not merely formal validity.
